item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an excellent example of why I chose to read the series from the 001 proposals up. This is a well written, decently scientific scip with a nice barb in the hook thanks to the idea of a group spreading the organism and possibly even having created it. This deserves a +1 from me and more people to have read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Parasites are a bit overdone, but the body horror is really disturbing.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone with both stomach problems and revulsion from larvae, I can't say it doesn't grabs me. I will actually have to wait to upvote simply because I have to focus myself to stand that photo.

But I think the first part could be much better. The science in it is interesting, but the presentation drags more than needed. Also, the fatality rate is unnecessarily high. Being a little less mortal wouldn't only be more realistic, it would also add to the horror with the idea of people living in such a debilitated state and experiencing the pain of starvation and self-digestion for far more time.

Less of the "you die" horror, and more of the body horror of digestive and feeding disorders being elevated to eleven.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure that the larvae of any beetle species actually has the apparatus to produce offspring, though I'm not confident enough of that to not upvote. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The process seems, as far as I can tell, to defy conservation of mass. Mass literally vanishes from withing an affected subject, which is impossible with the most advanced technology, nevermind normal insects.

Also, to a lesser extent, these don't progress past the larval stage; which is anomalous, even if it isn't supernatural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for the first problem, I'm not fixing that because more people complained when it was into specifics regarding the proximity to the user than trivial technicalities. If your problem is with such things, there are many other SCPs you will dislike.

For the second problem : "growths and relocate them to the nearest safe area, where they are drained and filled with eggs".
There's no acid left in the growth once they are harvested and drained, so they are completely safe for the hatchlings.

Third : In the containment they are given dead meat, which the adults are fully capable of breaking down, if for some reason they are incapable of tracking down their host (Which should be easy for them because of the over presence of grows with the insects saliva and hormone trails), it is stated in some form that the adults should be capable of breaking down any form of corpse or carcass with some form of success.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last line of containment technically says that all Foundation staff, everywhere in the world, have to wear hazmat suits at all times, forever.

The fridge-logic doesn't click for me. What keeps the hatchlings from portaling into the parent the same way the food did? Secondary point, if the parents take the growths away to a safe place before laying eggs, then the larvae aren't near any auto-digested slurry to feed off of. For that matter, this takes a while to kill. So there is every chance that the prey will be nowhere near a specimen when it finally succombs. What do the kids eat when all the bite victims are off thataway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took a while for this to move beyond "bug makes ew factor in people" to seriously anomalous. It's not really doing anything for me though, even with the implications of the final addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just give yourself some time to digest the content, then come back to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scientifically sound and disturbing as hell. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In 35% of cases, these micro-stomachs will also begin to spread to the esophagus and lungs of the subject, effectively drowning them in gastric acid.

I'm out, have your +1, we will never speak of this again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's something about this that I just can't… stomach. Not sure what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Discussed it in chat, fixed what few problems I found in the draft.
Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oddly enough, this is exactly why I /upvoted/. The bugs are anomalous, the fact that they can 'eat' nutrition lost by an SCP-1349-1 /miles/ away proved that, but it all makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's anomalous. The insect is absorbing the nutrients through the miniature stomachs it induces without being inside the afflicted organism. I still don't think these should always outright kill the host though. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's fairly well constructed, and has a pretty nice hook, but for whatever reason it just failed to grab me personally.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great goggley goodness, the body horror. And then the implications that these things are engineered! You had me at the beginning, and then you cranked up the horror from there. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't really care for this. I think. I'm not sure.
Seemed too… realistic. Non-anomalous.
Like, this could be a real bug, and it could do exactly that.
I don't know.
I downvoted, but I'm conflicted about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted at the reasonable scientific content, kept it when it got fucking horrifying and yet still plausible
